perturbation.original_text: 7. The Recipient agrees that the Receiver shall not disclose to the Recipient or any of the Recipient’s Representatives information about ident ifiable individuals forming part of the Confidential Information (" Personal Information ") unless required by the Recipient, acting reasonably, for the purpose of evaluating the Trans Action. If Personal Information is provided to the Recipient, then: (a) the Recipient shall comply with the Canada Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act , S.C. 2000, c. 5, as amended, and any similar prov incial legislation governing the protection of personal in formation in the private sector applicable to the Recipient in the course of collec ting, using and disclosing Personal Information in connection with the Transaction;
perturbation.changed_text: 7. The Recipient agrees that the Receiver shall not disclose to the Recipient or any of the Recipient’s Representatives information about ident ifiable individuals forming part of the Confidential Information (" Personal Information ") unless required by the Recipient, acting reasonably, for the purpose of evaluating the Trans Action. If Personal Information is provided to the Recipient, then: (a) the Recipient is not obligated to comply with the Canada Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act , S.C. 2000, c. 5, as amended, or any similar provincial legislation governing the protection of personal information in the private sector applicable to the Recipient in the course of collecting, using and disclosing Personal Information in connection with the Transaction;
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the recipient is not obligated to comply with PIPEDA, the modified text directly contradicts Canadian law regarding the protection of personal information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA mandates how private sector organizations collect, use, and disclose personal information. This modification states the Recipient is not obligated to comply with PIPEDA, directly contravening the law's mandatory requirements.

perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the recipient is 'not obligated to comply' with PIPEDA, which is a direct contradiction of the law. The snippet 'The Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)' indicates that PIPEDA is indeed a relevant law. The Perturbation Explanation also supports this by stating that the change 'directly contradicts Canadian law regarding the protection of personal information.'

perturbation.original_text: 9. The Receiver may elect at any time to terminate further access by the Recipient to the Confidential Information. If at any time the Recip ient determines not to proceed with the possible Transaction, the Recipient will promptly notify the Receiver in writing. Following any request by the Receiver or any of its Representatives, the Rec ipient agrees (i) to promptly re-deliver to the Receiver all written Confidential Information and a ny other written material containing or reflecting any of the Confidential Information in the possessi on of the Recipient or the Recipient's Representatives, (ii) the Recipient and the Recipie nt's Representatives will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part , mechanical or electronic, of such written material, and (iii) all Notes prepared by the Recip ient or the Recipient's Representatives will be destroyed, with all such destruction being confirme d by the Recipient to the Receiver in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The Receiver may elect at any time to terminate further access by the Recipient to the Confidential Information. If at any time the Recip ient determines not to proceed with the possible Transaction, the Recipient will notify the Receiver orally when they have time. Following any request by the Receiver or any of its Representatives, the Rec ipient agrees (i) to re-deliver to the Receiver written Confidential Information and any other written material containing or reflecting any of the Confidential Information in the possession of the Recipient or the Recipient's Representatives at their discretion, (ii) the Recipient and the Recipie nt's Representatives may retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part , mechanical or electronic, of such written material, and (iii) Notes prepared by the Recip ient or the Recipient's Representatives will be kept and shared at the Recipient's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for prompt written notification and the return/destruction of confidential information, which contradicts standard practices and legal expectations for confidentiality agreements and could violate laws related to trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: Trade Secrets Act, RSA 2000, c T-6
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the Recipient to retain and share confidential information at their discretion and removing the requirement to return or destroy notes, this modification weakens the protection of trade secrets and potentially violates the implied obligations to protect confidential information. Alberta's Trade Secrets Act provides a framework for protecting such information, and this modification undermines that protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Corporate, insolvency and competition law policy Corporate, insolvency and competition law policy The Corporate, Insolvency and Competition Policy Directorate ( CIC ) is responsible for the legislative policy development and review of a number of Canada's business framework laws and regulations in the corporate, insolvency, and competition policy areas. These include the following statutes and the regulations made under them: Statutory Review of the Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act and the Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act Comments received Consultation on the Canada Business Corporations Act Comments received Canada Not-for-profit Corporations Act Canada Business Corporations Act Canada Cooperatives Act Bankruptcy and Insolvency Act Companies' Creditors Arrangement Act Winding-up and Restructuring Act Competition Act Textile Labelling Act Consumer Packaging and Labelling Act Precious Metals Marking Act The CIC carries out its mandate by conducting policy analysis; monitoring domestic and international developments; undertaking research; consulting with stakeholders; supporting the legislative process; working to enhance public and stakeholder understanding; and, participating in international discussions and negotiations in its areas of responsibility. Report a problem Date modified: 2014-08-19

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract by removing the requirement for prompt written notification and the return/destruction of confidential information. This contradicts standard practices for confidentiality agreements, weakening trade secret protections. The Law Explanation states that this change undermines the protection afforded by Alberta's Trade Secrets Act. While the provided law snippets do not directly reference trade secret acts, the removal of obligations to protect and return confidential information creates a situation where trade secrets could be misappropriated, which is the situation that Trade Secret Acts seek to prevent.

perturbation.original_text: 11. Notwithstanding Section 9 or anything to the co ntrary in this Agreement, the Recipient and its Representatives are not required to destroy any com puter files containing the Confidential Information that are created during automatic compu ter system backup, provided that such files are stored securely by the Recipient and its Repres entatives, cannot be destroyed without undue efforts, and access to such files are limited. With respect to such backup computer files, the non- use and confidentiality obligations set forth in th is Agreement shall apply in perpetuity and survive expiration or termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Notwithstanding Section 9 or anything to the co ntrary in this Agreement, the Recipient and its Representatives are not required to destroy any com puter files containing the Confidential Information that are created during automatic compu ter system backup. With respect to such backup computer files, the non- use and confidentiality obligations set forth in th is Agreement will not apply.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the conditions of secure storage, difficulty of destruction, and limited access for backup files, along with eliminating the perpetual confidentiality obligations, conflicting with data protection principles and potentially violating privacy laws if the confidential information contains personal data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirements to securely store and limit access to backed-up confidential information and eliminating the application of confidentiality obligations to such files, the modification increases the risk of unauthorized access, use, or disclosure of personal information contained within those files, thus contradicting the principles of PIPEDA which mandates organizations to protect personal information under their control.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement to securely store and limit access to backed-up confidential information, and also eliminates the application of confidentiality obligations to such files. This directly contradicts the principles of PIPEDA, as explained in the Law Explanation, which mandates organizations to protect personal information under their control. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions PIPEDA requirements and the principle of 'safeguards', which are clearly undermined by the removal of secure storage and limited access requirements in the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: 21. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpr eted in accordance with the laws in force in the Province of Alberta. The Recipient hereby irrevoca bly attorns to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of the Province of Alberta for the determina tion of all matters arising hereunder in the event the Receiver should bring an action on this A greement in the Courts of the Province of Alberta. The Recipient hereby agrees that, notwith standing the foregoing, the Receiver may bring an action on this Agreement in any jurisdicti on where the Recipient has assets or in any jurisdiction where this Agreement has been breached or where a breach is threatened, and in such an event, the Recipient hereby irrevocably att orns to the jurisdiction of the Courts of such jurisdictions for the determination of all matters arising hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. This Agreement shall be governed by and interpr eted in accordance with the laws in force in the Province of Alberta. The Recipient hereby irrevocably attorns to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of the Province of Nunavut for the determina tion of all matters arising hereunder in the event the Receiver should bring an action on this A greement in the Courts of the Province of Alberta. The Recipient hereby agrees that, notwith standing the foregoing, the Receiver may bring an action on this Agreement in any jurisdicti on where the Recipient has assets or in any jurisdiction where this Agreement has been breached or where a breach is threatened, and in such an event, the Recipient hereby irrevocably att orns to the jurisdiction of the Courts of such jurisdictions for the determination of all matters arising hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By including attornment to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of the Province of Nunavut in addition to the courts of Alberta in a contract governed by Alberta law, this creates jurisdictional confusion and a potential conflict of laws. It's unlikely that a court in Alberta would enforce an exclusive jurisdiction clause for Nunavut, especially if the dispute has no connection to Nunavut.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alberta's choice of law principles regarding jurisdiction
perturbation.law_citation: Conflict of Laws Rules in Alberta
perturbation.law_explanation: Alberta courts generally respect choice-of-law clauses, but adding a second jurisdiction (Nunavut) to which the Recipient 'irrevocably attorns' creates ambiguity and potential conflict. If the contract is clearly governed by Alberta law, a clause mandating exclusive jurisdiction in Nunavut is unlikely to be enforced by Alberta courts, especially if the dispute lacks any connection to Nunavut. This contradicts the principle that choice of law and jurisdiction should be clear and unambiguous.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Conflict of laws - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Law for cases in multiple jurisdictions Conflict of laws Preliminaries Characterisation Choice of law Conflict of laws in the United States Incidental question Judicial interpretation Renvoi Public policy doctrine Hague Conference Definitional elements Jurisdiction Procedure Forum non conveniens Lex causae Lex fori Forum shopping Lis alibi pendens Connecting factors Domicile Lex domicilii Habitual residence Nationality Lex patriae Lex loci arbitri Lex loci rei sitae Lex loci contractus Lex loci delicti commissi Lex loci actus Lex loci solutionis Lex loci protectionis Proper law Lex loci celebrationis Choice of law clause Dépeçage Forum selection clause Substantive legal areas Status Capacity Contract Tort Marriage Divorce Property Forced heirship Hague Trust Convention Enforcement Enforcement of foreign judgments Anti-suit injunctions Revenue rule v t e Conflict of laws (also called private international law ) is the set of rules or laws a jurisdiction applies to a case , transaction , or other occurrence that has connections to more than one jurisdiction. [ 1 ] This body of law deals with three broad topics: jurisdiction , rules regarding when it is appropriate for a court to hear such a case; foreign judgments , dealing with the rules by which a court in one jurisdiction mandates compliance with a ruling of a court in another jurisdiction; and choice of law , which addresses the question of which substantive laws will be applied in such a case. [ 2 ] These issues can arise in any private law context, [ 2 ] but they are especially prevalent in contract law [ 3 ] [ 4 ] [ 5 ] [ 6 ] [ 7 ] [ 8 ] [ 9 ] [ 10 ] and tort law . [ 11 ] Scope and terminology [ edit ] The term conflict of laws is primarily used in the United States and Canada, though it has also come into use in the United Kingdom. Elsewhere, the term private international law is commonly used, [ 1 ] for example in Switzerland , the Federal Act on Private International Law (PILA) regulates which law should be applied when a dispute has connections with more than one jurisdiction. [ 12 ] Some scholars from countries that use conflict of laws consider the term private international law confusing because this body of law does not consist of laws that apply internationally, but rather is solely composed of domestic laws; the calculus only includes international law when the nation has treaty obligations (and even then, only to the extent that domestic law renders the treaty obligations enforceable). [ 13 ] The term private international law comes from the private law / public law dichotomy in civil law systems . [ 14 ] [ 15 ] In this form of legal system, the term private international law does not imply an agreed upon international legal corpus , but rather refers to those portions of domestic private law that apply to international issues. Importantly, while conflict of laws generally deals with disputes of an international nature, the applicable law itself is domestic law. This is because, unlike public international law (better known simply as international law ), conflict of laws does not regulate the relation between countries but rather how individual countries regulate internally the affairs of individuals with connections to more than one jurisdiction. To be sure, as in other contexts, domestic law can be affected by international treaties to which a country is party. Moreover, in federal states where substantial lawmaking occurs at the subnational level, notably in the United States, issues within conflict of laws often arise in wholly domestic contexts, relating to the laws of different states (or provinces, etc.) rather than of foreign countries. History [ edit ] Western legal systems first recognized a core underpinning of conflict of laws—namely, that "foreign law, in appropriate instances, should be applied to foreign cases"—in the twelfth century. [ 16 ] : 9–10 Prior to that, t
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of the Province of Nunavut in addition to Alberta, where the agreement is governed by Alberta law. This creates a jurisdictional conflict. The law explanation notes that 'adding a second jurisdiction (Nunavut) to which the Recipient 'irrevocably attorns' creates ambiguity and potential conflict' and that 'a clause mandating exclusive jurisdiction in Nunavut is unlikely to be enforced by Alberta courts, especially if the dispute lacks any connection to Nunavut.' This jurisdictional ambiguity, as confirmed by the LLM's Law Explanation, creates a contradiction that undermines the original exclusive jurisdiction clause, thus contradicting the principle of clarity in jurisdictional clauses.

perturbation.original_text: 22. This Agreement will continue indefinitely, unle ss otherwise agreed in writing by the Receiver.
perturbation.changed_text: 22. This Agreement will continue for 25 years, unle ss otherwise agreed in writing by the Receiver.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement to continue for 25 years contradicts standard confidentiality agreement practices.
perturbation.law_citation: Limitations Act RSA 2000 c L-12 s 3(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification sets a fixed term of 25 years. While this may seem reasonable, the Limitations Act RSA 2000 c L-12 sets the limitation period for bringing a legal claim is generally two years from the date the claim is discovered. By implication and common understanding, a contract promising confidentiality for 25 years will be held to that standard. This creates ambiguity regarding when a breach of contract claim can be brought. In practice, this could be seen as an unenforceable attempt to circumvent limitation rules.
perturbation.justification: The change from an indefinite agreement to a fixed 25-year term creates an inconsistency regarding the limitation period for legal claims. The Law Explanation points out that a fixed term could be seen as an unenforceable attempt to circumvent limitation rules, thus contradicting standard confidentiality agreement practices, as stated in the Perturbation Explanation, falling under the definition of Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction. Although there are no law snippets, the Law Explanation indicates that the modification potentially contradicts legal principles related to limitation periods for bringing legal claims.
